nodes.name: Ancient Civilizations
nodes.description: Major civilizations of the ancient world

items.prompt: Which ancient civilization built the pyramids at Giza?
items.explanation:
Nile Valley civilization, c. 3100–30 BCE
Built the Great Pyramid of Giza (c. 2560 BCE) — the only surviving Wonder of the Ancient World
Developed hieroglyphic writing, decoded via the Rosetta Stone in 1822 by Champollion
Mummification process preserved bodies for the afterlife; Tutankhamun's tomb discovered intact in 1922
Unified under Pharaoh Narmer/Menes around 3100 BCE into Upper and Lower Egypt
Cleopatra VII was the last active pharaoh before Roman annexation in 30 BCE
items.cardFront: Pyramids of Giza, Nile Valley, hieroglyphs

links.text: Pyramids of Giza, Nile Valley, hieroglyphs

items.prompt: Which ancient civilization was founded in 753 BCE and grew into an empire spanning three continents?
items.explanation:
Traditional founding date 753 BCE; Republic established 509 BCE; Empire from 27 BCE
At its height under Trajan (117 CE), controlled the entire Mediterranean, Britain to Mesopotamia
Engineered aqueducts, concrete, roads ('all roads lead to Rome'), and the Colosseum
Republic fell after Julius Caesar's assassination (44 BCE) and Octavian became Augustus, first emperor
Western Empire fell in 476 CE when Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus
Latin language became the root of all Romance languages (French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, Romanian)
items.cardFront: Colosseum, Latin, Republic to Empire, three continents

links.text: Colosseum, Latin, Republic to Empire, three continents

items.prompt: Which ancient civilization originated democracy in Athens and produced philosophers like Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle?
items.explanation:
City-states (poleis) flourished c. 800–146 BCE; Athens and Sparta were the dominant powers
Athenian democracy established by Cleisthenes c. 508 BCE — first known democratic system
Produced Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle — foundational Western philosophers
Alexander the Great (356–323 BCE) conquered from Greece to India, spreading Hellenistic culture
Originated the Olympic Games at Olympia in 776 BCE
Defeated the Persian Empire at Marathon (490 BCE) and Salamis (480 BCE)

items.prompt: Which ancient civilization, located between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, is often called the 'cradle of civilization'?
items.explanation:
Located in modern-day Iraq between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers
Sumerians (c. 4500–1900 BCE) invented cuneiform, the earliest known writing system
Produced the Epic of Gilgamesh, the oldest surviving great work of literature
Hammurabi of Babylon (r. c. 1792–1750 BCE) created one of the first written legal codes
Invented the wheel, the plow, the 60-minute hour, and the 360-degree circle
Successive empires: Sumerian, Akkadian, Babylonian, Assyrian, and Neo-Babylonian
items.cardFront: Tigris and Euphrates, cuneiform, 'cradle of civilization'

links.text: Tigris and Euphrates, cuneiform, 'cradle of civilization'

items.prompt: Which ancient civilization, founded by Cyrus the Great, created the largest empire the world had yet seen?
items.explanation:
Achaemenid Empire founded by Cyrus the Great c. 550 BCE in modern-day Iran
At its peak under Darius I, stretched from Egypt to India — largest empire the world had seen
Cyrus issued the Cyrus Cylinder (539 BCE), sometimes called the first declaration of human rights
Built the Royal Road (1,677 miles from Sardis to Susa) with a postal relay system
Defeated by Alexander the Great in 330 BCE, ending the Achaemenid dynasty
Zoroastrianism was the state religion — one of the world's oldest monotheistic faiths
items.cardFront: Cyrus the Great, largest ancient empire, Royal Road

links.text: Cyrus the Great, largest ancient empire, Royal Road

items.prompt: Which ancient Chinese dynasty (206 BCE–220 CE) established the Silk Road and is so influential that the majority ethnic group in China shares its name?
items.explanation:
Ruled 206 BCE–220 CE; one of China's longest and most influential dynasties
Established the Silk Road trade network connecting China to Rome
The ethnic majority in China (92%) is called 'Han Chinese' after this dynasty
Invented paper (c. 105 CE, attributed to Cai Lun), which revolutionized record-keeping
Adopted Confucianism as the state philosophy and created the civil service examination system
Preceded by the Qin Dynasty (221–206 BCE), which built the first Great Wall and unified Chinese script
items.cardFront: Silk Road, paper, 206 BCE–220 CE, ethnic majority namesake

links.text: Silk Road, paper, 206 BCE–220 CE, ethnic majority namesake

items.prompt: Which ancient Indian empire, founded by Chandragupta around 322 BCE, was the first to unify most of the Indian subcontinent?
items.explanation:
Founded by Chandragupta Maurya c. 322 BCE in Magadha (modern Bihar, India)
First empire to unify most of the Indian subcontinent, from Afghanistan to Bengal
Ashoka the Great (r. c. 268–232 BCE) converted to Buddhism after the bloody Kalinga War
Ashoka's edicts, carved on pillars and rocks, are among the oldest deciphered writings in India
The Ashoka Chakra (wheel) from his pillars appears on India's modern national flag
Kautilya (Chanakya), Chandragupta's advisor, wrote the Arthashastra — an ancient treatise on statecraft
items.cardFront: Chandragupta, Ashoka, first to unify India

links.text: Chandragupta, Ashoka, first to unify India

items.prompt: Which Mesoamerican civilization developed an advanced writing system, a base-20 number system with the concept of zero, and built pyramid-temples like Chichen Itza?
items.explanation:
Flourished c. 2000 BCE–1500 CE in present-day southern Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras
Developed the most sophisticated writing system in the pre-Columbian Americas (hieroglyphic)
Independently invented the concept of zero and used a base-20 (vigesimal) number system
Built Chichen Itza, Tikal, and Palenque — monumental pyramid-temple complexes
Created highly accurate astronomical calendars; the Long Count calendar sparked the '2012 apocalypse' myth
Never a single unified empire — organized as competing city-states, similar to ancient Greece
items.cardFront: Chichen Itza, hieroglyphs, concept of zero, Mesoamerica

links.text: Chichen Itza, hieroglyphs, concept of zero, Mesoamerica

items.prompt: Which Mesoamerican civilization built its capital Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco, on the site of modern-day Mexico City?
items.explanation:
Empire flourished c. 1300–1521 CE in central Mexico; also known as the Mexica
Capital Tenochtitlan built on an island in Lake Texcoco — population ~200,000, one of the world's largest cities
Used chinampas ('floating gardens') for agriculture on the lake
Practiced large-scale human sacrifice as central religious ritual
Conquered by Hernán Cortés and Spanish conquistadors in 1521 with help from rival indigenous groups
Tenochtitlan was demolished and rebuilt as Mexico City, now the capital of Mexico
items.cardFront: Tenochtitlan, Lake Texcoco, site of Mexico City

links.text: Tenochtitlan, Lake Texcoco, site of Mexico City

items.prompt: Which South American civilization built Machu Picchu and connected its empire with an extensive road system through the Andes?
items.explanation:
Empire flourished c. 1400–1533 CE along the Andes in South America; capital at Cusco (Peru)
Built Machu Picchu (c. 1450), a royal estate at 7,970 ft elevation, rediscovered by Hiram Bingham in 1911
Connected the empire with 25,000+ miles of roads (Qhapaq Ñan) through extreme mountain terrain
Used quipu (knotted strings) for record-keeping — had no written language
Largest empire in pre-Columbian America, stretching from Colombia to Chile
Conquered by Francisco Pizarro with ~168 Spanish soldiers in 1533 after capturing Emperor Atahualpa

items.prompt: Which ancient maritime civilization, based in city-states like Tyre and Sidon, created the alphabet that became the basis for Greek and Latin scripts?
items.explanation:
Maritime civilization on the coast of modern Lebanon, c. 1500–332 BCE
City-states included Tyre, Sidon, and Byblos (the word 'Bible' derives from Byblos)
Created a 22-letter alphabet that became the ancestor of Greek, Latin, Arabic, and Hebrew scripts
Master shipbuilders and navigators — dominated Mediterranean trade for centuries
Invented Tyrian purple dye, extracted from murex sea snails — so expensive it became synonymous with royalty
Founded Carthage (in modern Tunisia, c. 814 BCE), which later rivaled Rome in the Punic Wars
items.cardFront: Tyre and Sidon, invented the alphabet, Tyrian purple

links.text: Tyre and Sidon, invented the alphabet, Tyrian purple

items.prompt: Which ancient North African city-state, founded by Phoenician colonists, fought three wars against Rome before being destroyed in 146 BCE?
items.explanation:
Founded by Phoenician colonists c. 814 BCE in modern-day Tunisia
Fought three Punic Wars against Rome (264–146 BCE) — 'Punic' comes from Latin for Phoenician
Hannibal Barca famously crossed the Alps with war elephants to invade Italy in 218 BCE
Hannibal defeated Roman armies at Cannae (216 BCE), one of history's worst military defeats for Rome
Rome destroyed the city completely in 146 BCE; legend says they salted the earth
The phrase 'Carthago delenda est' ('Carthage must be destroyed') was Cato the Elder's rallying cry in the Roman Senate
items.cardFront: Hannibal, Punic Wars, North African rival of Rome

links.text: Hannibal, Punic Wars, North African rival of Rome
